author: @JillPenton
text:
Since the billionaires pay me less than a survival wage now, I can use all the help I can get.  
I bought  a reptile warmer pad and plug it in when I’m sitting doing crafting at my kitchen table in the winter.  I put my feet on it while set on low and that keeps me as warm as possible since the skin is thin.  I also put a sheepskin on the chair and I hat on my head because it’s the most effective at being warm. I also found an alpaca/wool child’s sweater felted it and made a pair of slippers with sheepskin insoles.  Be creative and learn the old skills as well as the new ones

author: @jamest.5001
text:
You can get all kind of little heaters  similar to that, I got some really cheap little pads, about 1.5-2" wide and about 5" long, with USB cables on them, they can go in gloves, jackets,  shoes,  put them on scooter or bicycle handlebars  about anything,  jd seal them between duck tape, to prevent corrosion,   seems they were less than  $4.00 and they work well,  use a couple with an old coat with some mylar and kapton tape, to seal them, put the  mylar space blanket piece 
 With the heater in the coat, with the heater towards the wearers body, with the mylar blanket  about the size of a notebook, or 2x that  depends on the persons size,  the heat will run you out of it in no time, it would be a well below freezing use only,   the blanket will reflect the heat back to the body,  it's only about 15 watts  for both  it would be much better at 12v with a controller,  because it takes a long time to get hot, it is possible 9 or 12v but ran in 5v ,
Maybe use a piece of aluminum foil used as a heat spreader, then cover with kapton tape, use the tiny heater to heat your whole back!    I have some 12x16. Pads, 12v with 3 levels, but gut it is auto shut off, after 10 minutes or something, and it is closer to 25-30 watts, it's a mystery mini electric blanket,   cool little things,  I build lithium battery packs for them,  enough to run it all night, if it would stayed on, maybe I can convince it to constantly stay on, until shut off manually, it atleast run for a few hours!   
Nice!!! ✌️

author: @1upPaintingAB
text:
Hey Robert! I have exactly the same seat cover but it runs on USB which saves a lot of effort, especially for those few without the means to do this (as simple as it is for most of us, think of the grandma's haha).
Super easy to run on a battery pack or solar generator, has a low, high, and off switch.
Mine only draws 5w on low and I think it was around 6.5 on high when I tested it. 

I do love this idea tho and it reminded me of an old 12v blanket I have that I may tinker with something similar.

God bless.

replies.author: @davidmowbray6352
replies.text: @royfokerpoker1802I took one apart to make a heated jacket. The heating element is probably nichrome wire which will bend the metal with repeated sitting causing increased resistance and hotspots. I took one apart to make a heated jacket. Carbon fibre makes a better more flexible element for heated seats and clothes you still want a fuse.

author: @chadblechinger5746
text: I love your videos, they are very informative. The most reliable way I have found to stay warm is the good ol classic hot water bottle.  It is simple and very effective. You want a metal one that can go in the fire and it needs to have a good lid or cap. The bigger the bottle the better within rational application. Metal water bottles are cheap, durable, can be recycled ( most likely already are) and you don't look like a twat carrying plastic preaching about the planet. Awesome clip and I will be using this in a modified igloo cooler to keep mushroom culture at incubation temps.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
